summary: Tashbih is the likening or assimilation of God to created things, typically applied to over-literal readings of scriptural descriptions of God's attributes. In Islamic theological discourse it functions largely as a term of critique and stands as the counter-pole to tanzih, the affirmation of God's transcendence.
description:
Tashbih (from a root meaning "to liken" or "to compare") denotes describing God in terms drawn from created things, attributing to Him bodily form, location, or human-like qualities. It is closely associated with tajsim (corporealism), the ascription of a body to God. Throughout classical kalam the word carried a pejorative charge: to be labeled a mushabbih ("assimilationist") was to be accused of compromising God's uniqueness.

The concept is best understood in tension with its counter-pole, tanzih, the declaration of God's absolute transcendence and freedom from resemblance to creation. Much of classical theology can be read as an attempt to hold the two in balance: to affirm the scriptural descriptions of God, His "hand," "face," and "establishment" upon the throne, without lapsing into either anthropomorphism (tashbih) on one side or, on the other, a stripping-away of the attributes (ta'til) that empties them of meaning.

The theological schools handled this tension differently. The Mu'tazila leaned strongly toward tanzih and read the anthropomorphic expressions figuratively (ta'wil), so as to deny God any bodily or spatial predicate. Traditionist and later Ash'ari and Maturidi theologians affirmed the attributes as reported while denying that they resemble creaturely attributes; a widely used formula is the affirmation of an attribute bila kayfa ("without asking how") and bila tashbih ("without likening"). The charge of tashbih was polemically directed at various groups, and its precise scope, which affirmations count as anthropomorphism and which as faithful acceptance of revelation, was itself contested among the schools.
editorial_notes: This entry frames tashbih through its standard pairing with tanzih, the way the two terms are conventionally treated jointly in the scholarly literature. It reports the pejorative use of the label and the differing school responses without endorsing any group's judgment about who was or was not a mushabbih, that boundary was itself a live polemical dispute and is deliberately left unadjudicated per the neutrality policy. The related terms tajsim (corporealism), ta'wil (figurative interpretation), and ta'til (negation of attributes) are glossed only briefly and are candidates for their own entries. A dedicated tanzih entry is assumed as the natural contrast target. A Muslim/Islamic scholarly primary text is retained for triangulation and balanced coverage (neutrality principles #7 and #8): Ibn Taymiyya's al-ʿAqīda al-Wāsiṭiyya, which articulates the traditionist rejection of tashbīh (bilā kayf, bilā tashbīh) from within the tradition, cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line. Per neutrality principle #9 the reference-work citations were removed (Encyclopaedia of Islam van Ess entry, Oxford Bibliographies Shah); to supply peer-reviewed scholarship alongside the primary creed, Wesley Williams's IJMES (2002) article on anthropomorphism in the creed of Ahmad ibn Hanbal was added.

relations.note: Tashbih (likening God to creation) is the counter-pole to tanzih (divine transcendence).
The dispute centers on how the divine names and attributes are to be understood.
Assimilating God to creation was held to compromise His absolute uniqueness.

historical_period.note: Debates over tashbih and tanzih shaped early and classical kalam from roughly the 8th century CE onward; no single origin date applies.

sources.citation: Williams, Wesley. "Aspects of the Creed of Imam Ahmad Ibn Hanbal: A Study of Anthropomorphism in Early Islamic Discourse." International Journal of Middle East Studies 34, no. 3 (2002): 441-463. Peer-reviewed study of tashbīh (anthropomorphism) and tanzīh in the creed attributed to Ahmad ibn Hanbal and early traditionalist discourse.
sources.license: Proprietary journal article; cited for reference, abstract and bibliographic record consulted, full text not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Ibn Taymiyya, Taqī al-Dīn Aḥmad. al-ʿAqīda al-Wāsiṭiyya. Classical Athari/traditionist creed requiring the revealed attributes be affirmed 'without asking how' (bilā kayf) and without likening God to creatures (bilā tashbīh / tamthīl), while equally rejecting the divesting of attributes (taʿṭīl).
sources.license: Public-domain classical text; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.
